profile.name: Henok Amdiye Endeshaw
profile.headline: Software Engineer | AI Developer | Mobile App Developer | ITSM Professional
profile.bio: I'm a software engineer based in Addis Ababa, building AI-powered, mobile-first, enterprise-grade systems. My background spans ITSM in banking, customer-facing platforms, and modern full-stack software. I care about shipping software that actually moves business metrics — not just code that compiles.

experiences.description: Lead incident, problem, and change management for a banking environment. Bridging engineering and operations — making sure the systems that move money stay up, audited, and improving.
responsibilities: Run incident response for production banking systems, owning P1 escalations end-to-end
Design and maintain the change advisory board process with risk-weighted approvals
Build SLA dashboards and service catalogues that leadership actually uses
Author post-incident reviews and drive corrective actions to closure
Integrate ITSM tooling with monitoring and CI/CD so change isn't a spreadsheet
achievements: Cut average P1 resolution time by 38% in the first year
Migrated the change management process from email-based to a structured workflow
Built an executive-facing SLA dashboard adopted across the IT org

experiences.description: Owned customer-facing operations for digital banking products — the on-the-ground view of what users actually need.
responsibilities: Resolved tier-2 customer escalations across digital channels
Translated customer feedback into actionable product requirements
Trained frontline agents on new digital banking features
achievements: Built a feedback-to-roadmap pipeline that fed 20+ product improvements
Achieved a 96% CSAT on handled tickets

experiences.description: Rotational program across network operations, BSS, and software engineering teams. Found my lane: software.
responsibilities: Shadowed senior engineers across network and software teams
Built internal tooling to automate repetitive operational tasks
Completed certification in network fundamentals and BSS
achievements: Automated a weekly reporting task, saving ~12 engineer-hours/week
Won the trainee cohort's innovation award

experiences.description: Building production software across web, mobile, and AI. Side projects became real products. Real products became revenue.
responsibilities: Design and ship end-to-end web and mobile applications
Build ML-powered features and integrate LLM APIs into production
Own architecture, deployment, monitoring, and the 2am pager
achievements: Shipped multiple production apps with thousands of active users
Built and deployed ML models for classification and recommendation
Open-source contributions in tooling and developer experience

testimonials.name: Daniel Bekele
testimonials.review: Henok is one of those rare engineers who can ship a feature, debug a production incident, and explain the architecture to a non-technical stakeholder — all in the same afternoon. He's the first person I call when something has to work on the first try.

testimonials.name: Sara Tesfaye
testimonials.review: We brought Henok in to stabilize our incident response. Within six months our MTTR dropped by a third and our change failure rate halved. He builds systems that survive contact with reality.

testimonials.name: Marcus Johnson
testimonials.review: I hired Henok to build the mobile app for our patient platform. He delivered a polished product that our users genuinely love. His attention to UX detail is unusual for a backend-leaning engineer.

projects.title: SmartDesk AI
projects.shortDescription: AI-powered ITSM platform that resolves tier-1 tickets autonomously.
projects.fullDescription:
SmartDesk AI is an enterprise-grade IT service management platform that uses LLMs and retrieval-augmented generation to resolve tier-1 tickets without human intervention. Built for a banking environment where speed and auditability both matter.

The system ingests historical ticket data, runbooks, and knowledge base articles into a vector store. When a new ticket arrives, an agent classifies it, retrieves the most relevant runbook, drafts a response, and either auto-resolves or escalates to a human with full context. Every action is logged for audit.

I designed the architecture end-to-end: the Next.js operator console, the FastAPI orchestration layer, the Postgres + pgvector store, and the CI/CD pipeline that ships models behind feature flags.
projects.architecture: Next.js 15 frontend on Vercel → FastAPI orchestration on ECS Fargate → Postgres + pgvector on RDS → S3 for document storage. Async event bus via SQS for ticket ingestion.
projects.lessonsLearned: The biggest lesson was treating the LLM as an unreliable component. Every prompt is wrapped in retry logic with structured outputs, and the system degrades gracefully to human-only when the model fails or returns low-confidence answers.
projects.challenges: Hallucination on edge-case tickets was the hard problem. Solved it with a combination of constrained decoding, RAG over runbooks, and a confidence threshold that forces human review below ~0.7.
projects.businessImpact: Resolved 42% of tier-1 tickets fully autonomously in the first month of pilot. Reduced average resolution time from 4.2 hours to 38 minutes for handled tickets.

media.caption: Operator console with real-time ticket queue

projects.title: Banking Mobile App Redesign
projects.shortDescription: Mobile-first banking experience with biometric auth and offline mode.
projects.fullDescription:
Redesigned and rebuilt the customer-facing mobile banking app from scratch. Native-quality UX with biometric login, offline transaction queuing, and a design system that ships features in days, not weeks.

The architecture uses React Native with a thin native layer for biometric flows and secure storage. Offline transactions are queued in SQLite and synced via a conflict-resolution protocol when connectivity returns. The design system is a single source of truth used across web and mobile.
projects.architecture: React Native app → REST/GraphQL gateway → Node.js services → Postgres primary with read replicas. Biometric auth via native bridge. Secure storage using Keychain (iOS) and EncryptedSharedPreferences (Android).
projects.lessonsLearned: Building a real offline-first app is mostly about designing the sync model correctly upfront. We spent more time on conflict resolution than on the UI — and that was the right call.
projects.challenges: Compliance: every transaction needs to be auditable end-to-end, and offline mode makes that non-trivial. Solved with a signed transaction queue that is replayed in order when online.
projects.businessImpact: Increased mobile DAU by 64% in the first quarter after launch. App store rating went from 2.8 to 4.6.

projects.title: Change Risk AI
projects.shortDescription: ML model that scores change requests by risk before they hit production.
projects.fullDescription:
Change Risk AI is a machine learning service that scores every change request submitted to the CAB on a 0–100 risk scale, with explanations. Trained on five years of historical change records, incidents, and post-mortems.

The model is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble wrapped in a FastAPI service, with a SHAP-based explanation layer so change managers can see *why* a change is flagged. A monitoring dashboard tracks calibration drift and retrains automatically when accuracy drops below threshold.
projects.architecture: FastAPI inference service on ECS → feature store in Postgres → training pipeline on SageMaker. SHAP explanations computed at inference time and surfaced in the Next.js CAB console.
projects.lessonsLearned: Explainability mattered more than raw accuracy. Change managers wouldn't act on the score unless they understood the reason. SHAP was non-negotiable.
projects.challenges: Class imbalance (most changes are low-risk). Solved with SMOTE + cost-sensitive learning and a calibration layer that maps raw scores to a 0–100 scale.
projects.businessImpact: Reduced change failure rate from 12% to 5% within six months. CAB meetings cut from 90 to 45 minutes.

media.caption: Risk scoring dashboard with SHAP explanations

projects.title: OpsBoard
projects.shortDescription: Executive SLA dashboard for IT operations — built to be used, not just admired.
projects.fullDescription:
OpsBoard is the executive-facing SLA dashboard for the IT org. Pulls data from ServiceNow, Grafana, and incident logs, and surfaces the metrics leadership actually cares about: availability, MTTR, change failure rate, top recurring incidents.

Designed to be the first tab opened in the morning — fast, dense, and honest. Built with Next.js and a streaming data layer so charts update in real time during incidents.
projects.architecture: Next.js dashboard → BFF aggregating ServiceNow + Grafana APIs → Postgres cache → WebSocket stream for live incident updates.
projects.lessonsLearned: An executive dashboard only works if it shows *one* thing per chart. Resist the urge to cram.
projects.challenges: Different data sources had different time zones and update cadences. Built a normalization layer that anchors everything to UTC and reconciles updates with a watermark.
projects.businessImpact: Adopted across the IT org. Cited as the single most useful internal tool in the next quarterly survey.

projects.title: KB Semantic Search
projects.shortDescription: Semantic search over the IT knowledge base — find the runbook in seconds.
projects.fullDescription:
Semantic search over an internal IT knowledge base of ~5,000 articles. Replaced keyword search with a sentence-transformer-based dense retrieval system. Users describe the problem in natural language and get the most relevant runbook, not just the one with the right keywords.

Built with sentence-transformers, FAISS, and a Next.js search UI that surfaces results with the matching section highlighted.
projects.architecture: sentence-transformers indexer runs nightly → FAISS index stored in S3 → FastAPI retrieval service → Next.js UI.
projects.lessonsLearned: Hybrid search (BM25 + dense) beat pure dense retrieval by a wide margin. Keyword signals still matter, especially for error codes and product names.
projects.challenges: Keeping the index fresh without rebuilding from scratch nightly. Used a delta-update pipeline that ingests only changed articles.
projects.businessImpact: Average time to find a runbook dropped from 6 minutes to 28 seconds. Self-service rate on tier-1 issues increased by 31%.

projects.title: DevTooling CLI
projects.shortDescription: Open-source CLI that automates the boring parts of shipping software.
projects.fullDescription:
An open-source command-line tool that automates the boring parts of shipping software: scaffolding, environment checks, deployment previews, and post-deploy verification. Written in Go for fast startup, distributed as a single static binary.

Used by ~2,000 developers across multiple teams. Featured on the GitHub trending list for a week.
projects.architecture: Single Go binary → pluggable subcommands → GitHub Action for CI integration.
projects.lessonsLearned: Documentation matters more than features. Adding clear examples tripled weekly active users.
projects.challenges: Cross-platform packaging without CI headaches. Settled on GoReleaser after two false starts.

posts.title: Building LLM Apps That Survive Production
posts.excerpt: Treat the model like any other unreliable dependency: timeouts, retries, structured outputs, and a graceful path back to humans.
posts.content:
# Building LLM Apps That Survive Production

Most LLM apps I've seen die in production the same way: the demo works, the first week in production works, and then one day the model hallucinates, the retry loop runs away, and the bill lands.

Here's the playbook I use now.

## 1. Treat the LLM as an unreliable dependency

Your LLM call should look like any other network call:

- **Timeouts**: hard upper bound, e.g. 5 seconds. The model doesn't get to decide when to answer.
- **Retries**: bounded, with exponential backoff, and a circuit breaker.
- **Structured outputs**: never trust free-form text. Force JSON, validate it, and reject if it doesn't parse.

## 2. Confidence thresholds beat vibes

The model tells you how sure it is. Use it. Below 0.7, escalate to a human with full context. Above 0.95, auto-execute. In between, queue for review.

## 3. Log everything

Every prompt, every completion, every token count, every cost. You'll thank yourself the first time something goes wrong.

## 4. Have a kill switch

A single env var that bypasses the model entirely and falls back to a hardcoded behavior. Test it in staging every deploy.

## 5. Cost is a feature

Track cost per request. Surface it on a dashboard. Set alerts. The model that costs $0.001 per call looks cheap until you do 10M of them.

---

LLMs are powerful, but they're not magic. The teams that win are the ones that ship them with the same engineering rigor they'd ship a database with.

posts.title: ITSM Is a Software Problem
posts.excerpt: The teams that treat incident management as a software problem — with code, data, and feedback loops — outperform the ones that treat it as a process problem.
posts.content:
# ITSM Is a Software Problem

There's a popular narrative that ITSM is about process: CAB meetings, RACI charts, ITIL books. That's true at the 30,000-foot view.

At ground level, ITSM is a software problem.

## What does that mean?

It means:

- Your incident playbook should live in code, not in a Word doc
- Your change requests should be API objects, not email threads
- Your post-mortems should be queryable, not PDFs in a folder
- Your SLA dashboards should be real-time, not monthly reports

## The teams that win

The teams that win at ITSM treat it like an engineering system. They:

1. **Automate the boring parts.** Ticket triage, on-call rotation, status pages — all automated.
2. **Measure everything.** MTTR, change failure rate, incident frequency, mean time to detect.
3. **Close the loop.** Every incident produces a corrective action. Every corrective action has an owner. Every action has a due date.
4. **Make data visible.** Dashboards that leadership actually opens.

## What changes

When you treat ITSM as a software problem, several things happen:

- Your on-call rotations get fair (no more "same three people")
- Your incidents get shorter (because the runbook is right there)
- Your changes get safer (because the risk is scored, not gut-feel)
- Your executives get honest (because the data is visible)

---

ITSM isn't a process you follow. It's a system you build.

posts.title: Why I Built This Portfolio (and What I Learned)
posts.excerpt: Notes from shipping a portfolio that's also a CMS, an AI demo, and a stress test of my own taste.
posts.content:
# Why I Built This Portfolio (and What I Learned)

Most engineer portfolios are static. This one isn't. It has:

- A real admin panel
- An AI assistant that knows my work
- A blog with a CMS
- Live analytics
- A 3D scene that doesn't tank performance on a 2019 MacBook

## The goal

When a recruiter lands here, they should feel that this is the work of someone who sweats the details. When a developer lands here, they should want to read the source.

## What I learned

1. **The admin panel is the product.** The home page is marketing; the admin is the engine. Building the admin first kept the rest honest.
2. **3D is a tax, not a feature.** Every Three.js line is a performance line. Be ruthless about what you render.
3. **The AI assistant is mostly retrieval.** Most questions about me are answered by my own content. RAG + a tight prompt is enough.
4. **Performance budgets aren't optional.** Lighthouse 100 is achievable if you decide it matters before you start.

## What's next

I'll keep writing here about the engineering behind the systems I build. If you want to follow along, the RSS feed is at `/feed.xml`.
